content.text:
*"Do you not know that in a race all the runners run, 
but only one gets the prize? Run in such a way as to get the prize."*{1 Corinthians 9:24}{Tree traversals—preorder, inorder, postorder—are fundamental to understanding recursive algorithms.}{Donald Knuth}
Trees power many fundamental algorithms in computer science.

A **binary search tree** (BST) stores 
ordered data for efficient search:

items: Left subtree contains values less than the root
Right subtree contains values greater than the root

content.title: BST operations
content.problem:
To search for value $x$: compare $x$ with root; go left if smaller, 
right if larger. Repeat until found or leaf reached.

Time: $O(h)$ where $h$ = height. Balanced trees have $h = O(\log n)$.

content.text:
A **decision tree** represents a sequence of 
binary decisions, each internal node being a question.

content.title: Sorting lower bound
content.problem:
Any comparison-based sorting algorithm can be modeled as a decision tree. 
Each leaf represents a permutation; there are $n!$ leaves. Height 
$\geq \log_2(n!) = \Omega(n \log n)$, proving sorting requires 
$\Omega(n \log n)$ comparisons.

content.text:
**Huffman coding** constructs optimal prefix-free 
binary codes for data compression.

content.title: Huffman's Algorithm
content.content:
1. Create a leaf for each symbol with its frequency.

2. Repeatedly combine the two lowest-frequency nodes under a new 
 parent with frequency = sum.

3. The resulting tree gives codes: left = 0, right = 1.

content.title: Huffman codes
content.problem:
Symbols $A, B, C, D$ with frequencies 0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1.

Combine $C(0.2)$ and $D(0.1)$ $\to$ node with 0.3.

Combine $B(0.3)$ and this node $\to$ node with 0.6.

Combine $A(0.4)$ and node with 0.6 $\to$ root with 1.0.

Codes: $A = 0$, $B = 10$, $C = 110$, $D = 111$.

Average length: $0.4(1) + 0.3(2) + 0.2(3) + 0.1(3) = 1.9$ bits.

content.text:
A **game tree** represents possible moves in a 
two-player game. The **minimax algorithm** finds optimal play.

content.title: Tic-tac-toe
content.problem:
The game tree for tic-tac-toe has the empty board as root; children are 
positions after one move; etc. Complete analysis shows optimal play 
results in a draw.

marginNotes.text:
She is a tree of life to those who take 
hold of her; those who hold her fast will be blessed.
